[08/09/2025, 9:42:33‚ÄĮAM] Twin Whore: #include <iostream>

int main()
{
    // The while(true) loop will make the program run indefinitely
    // until it is manually stopped by the user (e.g., using Ctrl+C).
    while (true)
    {
        // --- 1. Print a menu ---
        std::cout << "1: Print help" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "2: Print exchange stats" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "3: Place an ask" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "4: Place a bid" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "5: Print wallet" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "6: Continue" << std::endl;

        // --- 2. Read user input ---
        int userOption;
        std::cout << "Type in 1-6" << std::endl;
        std::cin >> userOption;

        // --- 3. Do something based on the user input ---
        // A switch statement is a clean way to handle multiple options.
        switch (userOption)
        {
        case 1:
            std::cout << "Help - choose options from the menu" << std::endl;
            std::cout << "and follow the on screen instructions." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 2:
            std::cout << "Exchange stats are not available yet." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 3:
            std::cout << "Placing an ask..." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 4:
            std::cout << "Placing a bid..." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 5:
            std::cout << "Wallet is empty." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 6:
            std::cout << "Continuing to next time step." << std::endl;
            break;
        default:
            // This block runs if the user enters a number that is not 1-6.
            std::cout << "Warning: Invalid option. Please type in a number from 1 to 6." << std::endl;
            break;
        }

        // Add a newline for better readability between menu loops.
        std::cout << std::endl;
    }

    // The program will technically never reach this point because of the infinite loop.
    return 0;
}

int main()
{
    // The while(true) loop will make the program run indefinitely
    // until it is manually stopped by the user (e.g., using Ctrl+C).
    while (true)
    {
        // --- 1. Print a menu ---
        std::cout << "1: Print help" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "2: Print exchange stats" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "3: Place an ask" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "4: Place a bid" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "5: Print wallet" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "6: Continue" << std::endl;

        // --- 2. Read user input ---
        int userOption;
        std::cout << "Type in 1-6" << std::endl;
        std::cin >> userOption;

        // --- 3. Do something based on the user input ---
        // A switch statement is a clean way to handle multiple options.
        switch (userOption)
        {
        case 1:
            std::cout << "Help - choose options from the menu" << std::endl;
            std::cout << "and follow the on screen instructions." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 2:
            std::cout << "Exchange stats are not available yet." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 3:
            std::cout << "Placing an ask..." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 4:
            std::cout << "Placing a bid..." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 5:
            std::cout << "Wallet is empty." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 6:
            std::cout << "Continuing to next time step." << std::endl;
            break;
        default:
            // This block runs if the user enters a number that is not 1-6.
            std::cout << "Warning: Invalid option. Please type in a number from 1 to 6." << std::endl;
            break;
        }

        // Add a newline for better readability between menu loops.
        std::cout << std::endl;
    }

    // The program will technically never reach this point because of the infinite loop.
    return 0;
}

int main()
{
    // The while(true) loop will make the program run indefinitely
    // until it is manually stopped by the user (e.g., using Ctrl+C).
    while (true)
    {
        // --- 1. Print a menu ---
        std::cout << "1: Print help" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "2: Print exchange stats" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "3: Place an ask" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "4: Place a bid" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "5: Print wallet" << std::endl;
        std::cout << "6: Continue" << std::endl;

        // --- 2. Read user input ---
        int userOption;
        std::cout << "Type in 1-6" << std::endl;
        std::cin >> userOption;

       
        switch (userOption)
   {
        case 1:
            std::cout << "Help - choose options from the menu" << std::endl;
            std::cout << "and follow the on screen instructions." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 2:
            std::cout << "Exchange stats are not available yet." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 3:
            std::cout << "Placing an ask..." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 4:
            std::cout << "Placing a bid..." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 5:
            std::cout << "Wallet is empty." << std::endl;
            break;
        case 6:
            std::cout << "Continuing to next time step." << std::endl;
            break;
        default:
           
            std::cout << "Warning: Invalid option. Please type in a number from 1 to 6." << std::endl;
            break;
        }

        std::cout << std::endl;
    }
return 0;
}

[22/10/2025, 7:03:44‚ÄĮPM] s: That error‚Äôs a classic Visual Studio Code IntelliSense meltdown, not an actual C++ compilation issue.

Here‚Äôs what‚Äôs happening:
	‚ÄĘ	It says #include errors detected... could not open source file "iostream" ‚ÄĒ basically, VS Code doesn‚Äôt know where your compiler‚Äôs system headers (like <iostream>) are.
	‚ÄĘ	This doesn‚Äôt mean <iostream> is missing. It means VS Code‚Äôs IntelliSense (the thing that gives squiggly lines and code suggestions) can‚Äôt find them in its configuration.
	‚ÄĘ	But your compiler (like g++, clang++, or MSVC) will still probably compile the code fine from terminal if your setup‚Äôs correct.

To fix the squiggly red nonsense:
	1.	Check your compiler:
	‚ÄĘ	If you‚Äôre using MinGW, make sure it‚Äôs installed (like in C:\MinGW or C:\Program Files\CodeBlocks\MinGW).
	‚ÄĘ	Add its bin folder to your PATH.
Example: C:\MinGW\bin
	2.	In VS Code:
	‚ÄĘ	Press Ctrl+Shift+P
	‚ÄĘ	Type: C/C++: Edit Configurations (UI)
	‚ÄĘ	Under Compiler path, choose your actual compiler ‚ÄĒ e.g.
C:\MinGW\bin\g++.exe
	‚ÄĘ	Under IntelliSense Mode, choose gcc-x64 (or similar).
	3.	Save and restart VS Code.
	‚ÄĘ	Once it reloads, the <iostream> error should disappear.

Also, small typo alert: your first line says

#inFlude <iostream>

That‚Äôs #include, not #inFlude. The red squiggles may be accurate for that part, to be fair.
